speaker: interviewer
text: It's July-- oh, I'm sorry. The date is February 21, 1997. And this is tape number four. We were-- when we had to cut off for the last-- for the end of the last tape, you were going through the chronology that you had gone back to Krasnostav. And then you were taken off the train. You were on your way to Chernivtsi, but taken off the train in Lemberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how long were you held in Lemberg after questioning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a few days in Lemberg, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did you get out of this jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they interrogated me there in Lemberg. And they gave me a very difficult time. They figured that I was spying against Russia. And they cannot imagine as a girl, a Jewish girl, should survive the war in the middle of Germany. They couldn't believe me. They couldn't-- so after they celebrated November 7, The October Revolution-- this is every November 7. It's a big celebration by them. Everybody get drunk, right? And the jail wasn't like a real jail with the bars and things like that. It's like a house arrest. I was under house arrest. I would say this would be the proper word.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I noticed it was cloudy, rain drizzling morning was it on the seventh. And the gate was open. And they brought in-- a big truck came in with boxes, maybe whiskey. I don't know what they brought into the jail. But they are at the place. And I took the opportunity. And I see the guy was standing there. He was shaking. He was-- it looked to me like he was drunk. I said, I take a chance. I'll take a chance. And I slipped slowly, slowly, slowly went towards the gate. And I run. And I run. And I run. And I looked if I figure maybe he's following me somewhere. But I went one, two, three blocks, and I didn't see nobody. So I figure, now I'm free already. And the people were very in a holiday mood. They were very-- the orchestra, I hear them playing 'The Internationale' and things like that. It was very, very gay in the streets, very lovely. And I run to there. And I ask people there, the train station. And they told me the-- and it's a matter of fact, I didn't have with me money, nothing. And I begged them on the street to please give me a kopek, give me a ruble. And I needed so badly. Everybody was in good mood. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came. Now this time, I went over straight to Chernivtsi. And nobody stopped me. That day is a special-- that day was an exceptional day. This is the biggest holiday by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if it hadn't been that holiday--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. If I would have been escaped. So that's what happened when I came to Chernivtsi. And the stores were open. And the streets were lit. And people were walking, like it's nothing, like-- I couldn't believe it. It's only a short time the war finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in-- a family took me, husband and wife took me to their house. They had only one son. It was he died during the war. He didn't have enough food. I don't know for what reason. And just they felt-- I told them my story. They felt very sorry for me. And they said to stay with us. And that's where I stayed for the entire year. And my husband used to come to buy and sell them certain things, merchandise. He used to come from another town from Poland to them and trade with them. And they knew my husband. There they introduced me to my husband. And it took only a few weeks, and we got married. And that was-- lived 38 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. So you met-- these people were complete strangers in Chernivtsi. And you go home with them. And they introduce you to your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. I told them the story of my life. They felt very sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And what about your husband's family? Had he lost family too?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had two brothers and a sister. And he lost them all plus his parents, and the relatives, and the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married on May 19th, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there with you? What was the setting like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He has this cousin, a lady who survived the war with two small children. One child, when I met him, he must have been about three or four-- skinny. He looked like one-year-old, only bones and skin. And the older boy, the older brother was about four years older. Must have been four and eight or four and nine, something like that, when I met him. And she herself, her name was Rosa. And she threw the boys from a running train. She threw them off. She tore off a board. Maybe it wasn't good attached. I don't know. And she threw the boys on the floor, on the ground. And she jumped after them. And some of the Gestapo was aiming on one of the boys. He wounded him on his leg. She tore off his shirt. She made a bandage, and they tried to stay in there, lie on the ground for a few hours. And then she-- when everything was-- he felt better, the boy, she took the two boy, little boys. And she went with them back to her village. She went very bad, even worse than I. Because it was difficult with two little boys to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: With children, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she died in 1967. Of course, the boys have their passport now. They are in New York. And they're doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after you married? Where did this young couple who had come together after the war go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tried immediately to contact my uncles in the United States through a paper. I didn't even know their real English names. I know the ways they used to call them in the Europe, the Jewish names. By the paper helped me find them. They did send immediately affidavits for us. And then we have to sit and wait for a quarter in a DP camp until we were ready to come here in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- which DP camp were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Castle, in a DP camp. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did you do while you were there? Did you have specific jobs that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was trying to tutor some children there. Because it wasn't too many children, only a few survivors, very, very few. And that we were waiting, mainly waiting for the visa to be ready to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came here in May-- no, in April of 1948. My daughter was born there. In the DP camp, she was born there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Born there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Were there a lot of children being born in that DP camp? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of them, yes, some.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some children were born there. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you first come in the United States? Where were your uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York-- my uncles, they were waiting already. They were waiting on the pier to pick us up. And of course, the first thing what we started to make a living. They go to work. We didn't-- my uncle didn't want to get relief money. He said he's ashamed we should take money from the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he trained my husband. He had a store, my uncle, a cafeteria store in New York. Restaurant business-- it was a restaurant business. So he took right away my husband to teach him a trade, a new trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later I start working also as a cashier in his place when the girl was a little older.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and is this the career that your husband had most of his life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was in the restaurant business with this uncle, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was in the restaurant business. Other than working and raising the children, is there anything else about your life in the United States that-- the time after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. I went to school. I made high school, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I couldn't go further, because I had already my other child. My son was born after that, so I couldn't. I had to stop. And then I was working until I retire. Only about 10 years ago, I retire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you move to Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've been going to Florida on and off for a few years. But we moved-- I'm seven years in this building.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you came here permanently seven years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you want to tell us about your children, what they're doing now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my children are-- thank god for that. My son is a chief psychiatrist in veteran's hospital in New York. His wife is a MD too. And my daughter has her master degree from Columbia in mathematics. She's a math teacher in high school in Bayside since 1971 in the same school teaching math.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so is her husband. So thank god, the children are very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren, you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four--

speaker: interviewer
text: Four.

speaker: interviewee
text: --two from the daughter and two from the son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You've written a book about your experiences. And in that book, you talk about a diary that you were keeping during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was obviously very important to you, even when all of this was happening to you, to keep a record of it. How did you manage to keep that diary?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the diary I put only numbers, dates. I wouldn't put it in words. Because in case if somebody finds it, it would have been in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have dates, like when was the action in Krasnostav in August 29, 1941, and so forth. I have dates there. And from the dates, I put it in the book. And of course, I have many pictures here in the United States by my uncles. And they gave me the pictures back. So I have pictures from my parents, from my grandparents, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did anyone else survive from your shtetl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope, nobody. As a matter of fact, when I came back to Iablonovka after the war, to Anne Maria, and I ask her. So she said, you are the only survivor. That's what she told me. Of course, I have here people who came in 1906. The first people from Krasnostav came to the United States in 1906. Then their name is Goldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came other people in 1921. He brought over other people. But I haven't-- I've been in touch with them. Some of them died already, the older ones. But the younger generation, the younger people, I've been in touch with. And we sometimes, when we get together, we're reminiscent about the good old days. They had a very difficult life here in the United States when they came. They went in window washing business-- very difficult. It wasn't good. And because after it the Depression years later, they didn't have as much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. So you obviously felt it was very important to--a as the only survivor of Krasnostav--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to tell that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything in our interview today, anything that you think you've forgotten that you'd like to go back and add at this point that comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I want to add one point is this-- how rough the Communists treated us for my younger years. I was in school in that time. I must have been 10 years old. And they came. They came. And somebody told them that my father has dollars, American dollars. And they searched the house. And they did find about $10 or $20 in a bottle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took my father away to jail-- for what? Because he had a few dollar, American dollars? He came back like a skeleton from the jail. So that's what it was in my mind. It's so engraved in my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, the years when I was a student, that was also another episode. I once went to a store. And I bought the polo shirts, white with blue colors. And I figure, I'll send them home, about 10 or 15 shirts that my mother will trade them. And she'll send me back the money, because I needed it. They did find it in the dormitory. I put it-- before I put it to the post office, I put them under the bed. They did find them. And the director from the school, he gave me a big argument.

speaker: interviewer
text: He called me to his office. And he said, what are you, you [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? You're not supposed to deal like this, you know? We are Communist government. We don't sell like the black market and things like that. You're not supposed to do this. He gave me a big argument. What other episodes? So you've dealt with-- just in bringing this to a close, what seems so significant about your experience is that you dealt not only with the Holocaust and the anti-Semitism, but with the problems of communism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Communism-- that is the worst. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say-- and you obviously felt it was very important to tell your story. You've written a book. What would you most like young people, the next generation, to know about this period of time in history?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say about the young years, the early years of my life, being Russian, I didn't realize at that time. Now I know better while I'm getting older that the communism is-- they take away the freedom from the people, the freedom from religion, freedom of speech. They treated the people very, very bad. It's no comparison. As I compare life in Russia, the life here in the United States, the freedom that I am enjoying here with my family, it's tremendous. It's unbelievable to describe this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that we should not take our freedom for granted, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: For granted-- we should bless every day what we live in a free country.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. On behalf of the foundation, I'd like to thank you for making the interview today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope some people will learn. The young generation should learn about this. They should know about what the communism means and what the fascism means. They should be blessed to live in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. To the left is my mother Hannah Meister. In the middle, I am seven or eight years old. And to the right is my father Abraham Meister. This was taken in Krasnostav. I was that time around seven years old, seven or eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother Malka Meister from Annapole. This picture must have been taken in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. I was in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in a teacher's college. Must have been in 1937. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is the picture taken in Germany around 1943. It's the [SPEAKING GERMAN] where Herr [? Schwertzel, ?] he was the doctor from the barrack. And I was with another girl coworker, Maria. This is a wedding picture, my wedding picture-- my husband Abraham Rubinstein-- taken in 1946, May 19th, in Germany. To the right is Rosa Mayer Tenenbaum, a cousin of my husband. And this is the two little boys. She survived with them the war. And that's my husband and myself to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken after the war in Chernivtsi. This picture was taken in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] while I was a student in the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], in teachers college. It must have been in 1938 or '39. To the right is Uncle George Meister. And to the left is Aunt Blanche Meister. This is the uncle of my late father. He lived in the United States. And he did send us affidavits to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been the year after they married. Or this is a wedding picture-- close to the wedding picture. This picture was taken in Flushing around 1971. This my husband Abraham Rubinstein to the left. And I'm to the right, Donna Rubinstein. This is Helen Spiegel, Helen Rubinstein Spiegel, a school teacher in Bayside High School. She's teaching since 1970-- math teacher.
